Uploading Dataset¶

train = pd.read_csv('train.csv')

Identifying missing values¶

In [31]:
df.isnull().sum().sort_values(ascending=False)
Out[31]:

owns_car                   369
no_of_days_employed        315
yearly_debt_payments        62

migrant_worker              53

credit_limit_used(%)         0

occupation_type              0
name                         0
net_yearly_income            0
owns_house                   0
gender                       0
age                          0

credit_card_default

credit_card_default

After reshaping,the value_count for credit_card_default remains almost the same. 0(non-defaulters) is 91.79% and 1(defaulter) 8.21%
In [ ]:

Handling missing values(using median,mode)¶

Fill NAN for Categorical features¶

In [34]:
df[cat_col].isnull().sum()
Out[34]:
customer_id          0
name                 0
gender               0
owns_car           369
owns_house           0
occupation_type      0
dtype: int64

    print("Column Name:",i)

    print("\n")

occupation_type
Unknown                  0.314500
Laborers                 0.178200
Sales staff              0.103000
Core staff               0.089900
Managers                 0.069033
Drivers                  0.060767
High skill tech staff    0.037567

Name: proportion, dtype: float64
As there are missing values mostly less than 2%, so, let us impute all missing values with statistical way. FillNA by MODE - categorical variable and FillNA by MEDIAN - numerical variable
In [ ]:

FillNA by MODE - categorical variable¶

Gender¶

In [36]:
df['gender'].value_counts()
Out[36]:
gender
F      19731
M      10268
XNA        1
Name: count, dtype: int64
In [37]:
## Replacing XNA with M

df['gender'] = df['gender'].apply(lambda x: 'M' if x=='XNA' else x)
df['gender'].value_counts()
Out[37]:
gender
F    19731
M    10269
Name: count, dtype: int64

Owns_Car (cat_col)¶

In [38]:
df.fillna({'owns_car':df['owns_car'].mode()[0]},inplace=True)
df['owns_car'].value_counts(normalize=True)
Out[38]:
owns_car
N    0.665033
Y    0.334967
Name: proportion, dtype: float64
In [39]:
df[cat_col].isnull().sum()
Out[39]:
customer_id        0
name               0
gender             0
owns_car           0
owns_house         0
occupation_type    0
dtype: int64
In [ ]:

FillNA by MEDIAN - Numerical Column¶

In [40]:
df[num_col].isnull().sum().sort_values(ascending=False).head(10)
Out[40]:
no_of_children          502
no_of_days_employed     315
yearly_debt_payments     62
total_family_members     56
migrant_worker           53
credit_score              6
age                       0
net_yearly_income         0
credit_limit              0
credit_limit_used(%)      0
dtype: int64

no_of_children(num_col)¶

In [41]:
df.fillna({'no_of_children': df['no_of_children'].mode()[0]}, inplace=True)
int(df['no_of_children'].isna().sum())
Out[41]:
0

no_of_days_employed (num_col)¶

In [42]:
# impute 'no_of_days_employed' with median based on 'occupation_type'

occp_days_employed = df.groupby(by='occupation_type')['no_of_days_employed'].median()

for i in occp_days_employed.index:
    df['no_of_days_employed'] = np.where((df['no_of_days_employed'].isna()) & (df['occupation_type'] == i), occp_days_employed[i], df['no_of_days_employed'])

int(df.no_of_days_employed.isna().sum())
Out[42]:
0

yearly_debt_payments (num_col)¶

In [43]:
### impute 'yearly_debt_payments' with median based on 'credit_card_default'

yearDebt_credit = df.groupby(by='credit_card_default')['yearly_debt_payments'].median()
for i in yearDebt_credit.index:
    df['yearly_debt_payments'] = np.where((df['yearly_debt_payments'].isna()) & (df['credit_card_default'] == i), yearDebt_credit[i], df['yearly_debt_payments'])

int(df.yearly_debt_payments.isna().sum())
Out[43]:
0

total_family_members(num_col)¶

In [44]:
# imputing with mode of the data

df.fillna({'total_family_members': df['total_family_members'].mode()[0]}, inplace=True)

#check null values count
int(df['total_family_members'].isna().sum())
Out[44]:
0

migrant_worker(num_col)¶

In [45]:
# imputing with mode of the data
df.fillna({'migrant_worker': df['migrant_worker'].mode()[0]}, inplace=True)

# check null values count
int(df['migrant_worker'].isna().sum())
Out[45]:
0

credit_score(num_col)¶

In [46]:
# check medians of credit_score wrt credit_card_default
cred_score = df.groupby(by='credit_card_default')['credit_score'].median()

for i in cred_score.index:
    df['credit_score'] = np.where((df['credit_score'].isna()) & (df['credit_card_default'] == i), cred_score[i], df['credit_score'])

# check for null values in 'credit_score'
int(df['credit_score'].isna().sum())
Out[46]:
0
In [47]:
df[num_col].isnull().sum().sort_values(ascending = False)
Out[47]:

no_of_children             0

credit_score               0
prev_defaults              0

# cat and num col
    
df.isnull().sum()

In [ ]:

Concentrated EDA¶

(Key features - credit_card_default, age, credit_score, credit limit percentage,occupation,gender)¶

In [49]:
# Ploting Credit_default ratio

total_default_values = df.credit_card_default.value_counts(ascending=True).values

plt.figure()
plt.pie(total_default_values, labels=['Defaulter', 'Non Defaulter'], autopct='%1.1f%%', shadow=True)
plt.title('Defaulter Ratio', fontsize=18)
plt.show()
The pie chart shows the distribution of the target variable - 'credit_card_default' Non Defaulter: 91.8% (Majority customers) Defaulter: 8.2% (Very few customers) Reflecting a highly imbalanced dataset.
In [ ]:
In [50]:
# visualize AGE feature - histplot

plt.figure()
sns.histplot(df['age'], kde=True)
plt.show()
-Most ages lie between 23 to 55. -The distribution looks - uniformly spread,no strong skewness,no extreme outliers visible -There is only 1 noticeable spike near 55,as many customers fall in that age hich might be because of age capping during preprocessing, synthetic/generated data rounded values.
In [ ]:
In [51]:
# boxplot of credit_defaulter-credit_score

plt.figure(figsize=[7,6])
sns.boxplot(x=df['credit_card_default'], y=df['credit_score'],hue=df['credit_card_default'])
plt.show()
Non-defaulters (class 0) have higher credit scores with a Median credit score ≈ 800 Scores mostly range between 720 – 880 indicating financially reliable customers. Defaulters (Class 1) have lower credit scores with a Median credit score ≈ 600 Scores mostly range between 550 – 650 suggesting a higher financial risk. Therefore Lower credit score → Higher default probability Higher credit score → Lower default probability
In [ ]:
In [52]:
# boxplot of credit_defaulter-credit_limit_utilization

plt.figure(figsize=[7,6])
sns.boxplot(x=df['credit_card_default'],y=df['credit_limit_used(%)'],hue=df['credit_card_default'])
plt.show()
-Defaulters box (class 1) is positioned significantly higher on the y-axis compared to Non-defaulters(class 0) indicating that the individuals who default uses higher percentage of their credit limit. -Defaulters show median of 85% which is quite high compared to the median of Non-defaulters 50%. -The box plot reflects a strong positive correlation between credit limit utilization and default risk. -Higher credit limit utilization is one of the key factors for prediciting the credit card defaults.
In [ ]:
In [53]:
# clustered horizontal barplots for occupation types wrt 'net_yearly_income' and 'credit_default'

plt.figure(figsize=[18,8])
sns.barplot(x=df['occupation_type'], y=df['net_yearly_income'], hue=df['credit_card_default'])
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()
Income Variation - People with Occupations like Managers, RealtyAgents, IT staff,Drivers show a high income ranging from Rs 2,00,000 to Rs 3,25,000. While Cleaning staff,Hr staff,cooking staff reflect a low income people. With occupation such as Managers, RealtyAgents, IT staff and Drivers,the average income for those who defaulted is slightly less from those didnt.High earned annual income reflects less default rate of EMI's. Error bars(thin black lines at the end of each bar) - long error bars indicate high variablility/small sample size/avg value less reliable short error bars - avg income is more concentrated and consistent Therefore, borrower's(low) income level and occupation_type make them a high risk candidate for a credit card.
In [ ]:
In [54]:
# male or female defaulters

plt.figure(figsize=[7,6])
sns.countplot(x=df['gender'], hue=df['credit_card_default'])
plt.show()
The above barchart illustrates a class imbalance comparing the count of individual who are non defaulters (class 0-blue) and defaulters(class 1-orange) across the gender category - male and female.
In [ ]:
In [55]:
# boxplot of all continuous variables

plt.figure(figsize=[16, 12])
plt.subplot(2,3,1)
df['net_yearly_income'].plot.box()
plt.title('net_yearly_income Box Plot')

plt.subplot(2,3,2)
df['no_of_days_employed'].plot.box()
plt.title('no_of_days_employed Box Plot')

plt.subplot(2,3,3)
df['yearly_debt_payments'].plot.box()
plt.title('yearly_debt_payments Box Plot')

plt.subplot(2,3,4)
df['credit_limit'].plot.box()
plt.title('credit_limit Box Plot')

plt.subplot(2,3,5)
df['credit_score'].plot.box()
plt.title('credit_score Box Plot')
plt.show()
Net_yearly_incomeBox Plot - most of the data is concentrated at the lower end of the scale(0). There exists a significant amount of outliers. Data is highly right skewed. no_of_days - most of the data is concentrated between 0 to 50000 days.There is a massive outlier near (350,000 days roughly - 958 years). yearly_debt_payments - Bulk debt payments fall below 50,000.There are several outliers ranging from 100,000 to 300,000. credit_limitbox - most of the data is concentrated at lower end (under 100,000).There are numerous outliers extending upto 800,000 credit_score_boxplot -this is more normally distributed and balanced with a median around 750.No outliers exist. The above variables are heavily skewed by outliers.Scaling or capping of these outliers is necessary before building the ML Model
In [ ]:

Data Processings¶

WOE Binning (grouping categorical variables into discrete categories and replacing them with a calculated WOE score)¶

In [56]:
selected_vars = ['customer_id', 'name', 'age', 'gender', 'owns_car', 'owns_house', 
                  'no_of_children', 'net_yearly_income', 'no_of_days_employed', 'occupation_type',
                  'total_family_members', 'migrant_worker', 'yearly_debt_payments', 'credit_limit', 
                  'credit_limit_used(%)', 'credit_score', 'prev_defaults', 
                  'default_in_last_6months', 'credit_card_default']
df_woe = df[selected_vars]
In [57]:
print(df_woe.dtypes)

customer_id                 object
name                        object
age                          int64
gender                      object
owns_car                    object
owns_house                  object
no_of_children             float64
net_yearly_income          float64
no_of_days_employed        float64
occupation_type             object
total_family_members       float64
migrant_worker             float64
yearly_debt_payments       float64
credit_limit               float64
credit_limit_used(%)         int64
credit_score               float64
prev_defaults                int64
default_in_last_6months      int64
credit_card_default          int64
dtype: object
In [58]:
df_woe = df[selected_vars].copy()
In [59]:
# convert categorical columns explicitly

df_woe['occupation_type'] = df_woe['occupation_type'].astype('category')
df_woe['gender'] = df_woe['gender'].astype('category')
In [60]:
df = df_woe.drop(columns=['customer_id', 'name'])
In [61]:
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", category=FutureWarning)
In [62]:
bins = sc.woebin(df, y='credit_card_default')

[INFO] creating woe binning ...

C:\Users\Home\anaconda3\Lib\site-packages\scorecardpy\condition_fun.py:40: UserWarning: Could not infer format, so each element will be parsed individually, falling back to `dateutil`. To ensure parsing is consistent and as-expected, please specify a format.
  datetime_cols = dat.apply(pd.to_numeric,errors='ignore').select_dtypes(object).apply(pd.to_datetime,errors='ignore').select_dtypes('datetime64').columns.tolist()
C:\Users\Home\anaconda3\Lib\site-packages\scorecardpy\condition_fun.py:40: UserWarning: Could not infer format, so each element will be parsed individually, falling back to `dateutil`. To ensure parsing is consistent and as-expected, please specify a format.
  datetime_cols = dat.apply(pd.to_numeric,errors='ignore').select_dtypes(object).apply(pd.to_datetime,errors='ignore').select_dtypes('datetime64').columns.tolist()

# visualizing the Weight of Evidence (WOE) bins created by woebin().

sc.woebin_plot(bins)

In [ ]:

Calculating Info_value¶

In [67]:
import scorecardpy as sc
In [68]:
# Calculate IV using credit_card_default as target
iv_df = sc.iv(df,y='credit_card_default')

# Sort IV values from highest to lowest
iv_df = iv_df.sort_values(by='info_value',ascending=False)

print(iv_df)

low_iv_cols = iv_df.loc[iv_df['info_value'] < 0.02,'variable'].tolist()

print(low_iv_cols)

['age', 'yearly_debt_payments', 'migrant_worker', 'no_of_children', 'total_family_members', 'credit_limit', 'owns_car', 'net_yearly_income', 'owns_house']

In [73]:
df["gender"] = df["gender"].apply(lambda x : 1 if x == "F" else 0)

Creating dummies of occupation_type¶

In [74]:
occupation_type_encoded_df = pd.get_dummies(df["occupation_type"], prefix= "occupation")
df = pd.concat([df, occupation_type_encoded_df], axis=1)
df.drop(columns=["occupation_type"], inplace=True)

Splitting Data¶

In [75]:
X = df.drop(['credit_card_default'], axis=1)
y = df['credit_card_default']

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.3, stratify=y, random_state=42)

print("X=",X_train.shape, X_test.shape)
print("y=",y_train.shape, y_test.shape)

# WOE data

X_woe = train_woe.drop(['credit_card_default'], axis=1)

X_train_woe, X_test_woe, y_train_woe, y_test_woe = train_test_split(X_woe, y_woe, test_size=0.3, stratify=y_woe, random_state=42)

Scaling data¶

In [81]:
# scaling the features to standardize
scaler = MinMaxScaler()

cols = X_train.columns
X_train[cols] = scaler.fit_transform(X_train[cols])
X_test[cols] = scaler.transform(X_test[cols])
In [ ]:

Data Imbalancing Handling¶

In [82]:
from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE
In [83]:
# initialize SMOTE method

sm = SMOTE(random_state=42)
X_train,y_train = sm.fit_resample(X_train,y_train)

# performance ks & roc ------

test_perf = sc.perf_eva(y_test_woe, test_pred, title = "test")
In [92]:
# score ------
card = sc.scorecard(bins, lr, X_train_woe.columns)

old_df = old_df.copy()

# credit score
score = sc.scorecard_ply(old_df, card, only_total_score=False, print_step=0, replace_blank_na=True, var_kp = None)

30000 rows × 12 columns

In [94]:
plt.hist(score["score"])
Out[94]:
(array([ 1503.,   629.,   407.,  1204.,   437.,  5800.,  2788.,   645.,
        13398.,  3189.]),
 array([ 169.,  259.,  349.,  439.,  529.,  619.,  709.,  799.,  889.,
         979., 1069.]),
 <BarContainer object of 10 artists>)
- The data is heavily skewed towards the higher end of the scale.There are 2 major clusters - one between 600-750 and another much larger dominant spike around 900s, which typically correlate with lower credit risk. - This suggests that the majority of the individuals in this dataset have high scores, which typically correlates with lower credit risk. - There is a much smaller concentration of data point in the lower range(200 to 500),representing a minority of high-risk entries.
In [ ]:

Evaluation matrix¶

In [95]:
# evaluation function

def evaluation(model):
    y_train_pred = model.predict(X_train)
    y_test_pred = model.predict(X_test)
    print("="*50)
    
    print(confusion_matrix(y_train, y_train_pred))
    print(classification_report(y_train, y_train_pred))
    print("Accuracy of TRAIN data:", 100*accuracy_score(y_train, y_train_pred))
    print("F1_Score of TRAIN data:", 100*f1_score(y_train, y_train_pred, average="macro"))
    print("="*50)
    
    print(confusion_matrix(y_test, y_test_pred))
    print(classification_report(y_test, y_test_pred))
    print("Accuracy of TEST data:", 100*accuracy_score(y_test, y_test_pred))
    print("F1_Score of TEST data:", 100*f1_score(y_test, y_test_pred, average="macro"))
    print("="*50)
    
    # calculate the RMSE
    rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_test, y_test_pred))
    print("RMSE: ", rmse)
    
    # calculate ROC AUC
    roc_auc = roc_auc_score(y_test, y_test_pred, average=None)
    print("ROC AUC score: ", roc_auc)

Random Forest¶

In [101]:
# initialize and fit the model

regr_rfr = RandomForestClassifier(random_state=42, oob_score=True)
regr_rfr.fit(X_train, y_train)

CatBoost¶

In [103]:
# Initialize and fit the model

reg_catb = CatBoostClassifier(random_seed=42)
reg_catb.fit(X_train, y_train)

XGBoost¶

xgb_model = XGBClassifier(random_seed=42)
xgb_model.fit(X_train, y_train)

In [ ]:

KNN¶

In [112]:
## Iterating knn model for all values of k from 1-39

error_rate = []

for i in range(1,40):
    knn = KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=i)
    knn.fit(X_train, y_train)
    pred_i = knn.predict(X_test)
    error_rate.append(np.mean(pred_i != y_test))
In [113]:
# ploting the error_rate

plt.figure(figsize=[10,6])
plt.plot(range(1,40), error_rate, color='blue', linestyle='dashed',
          marker='o', markerfacecolor='red', markersize=10)
plt.title('Error rate vs K')
plt.xlabel('K')
plt.ylabel('Error Rate')
Out[113]:
Text(0, 0.5, 'Error Rate')
In [114]:
# # building knn and fit the model

knn = KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors = 2)
knn.fit(X_train, y_train)
Out[114]:

In [ ]:
Conclusion: - Random Forest, Decision Tree, LightGBM, KNN, CatBoost and XGBoost models performed well with almost the same accuracy of 0.96 However minor difference exsit in F1_Score, precision and recall. - The most significant variables in predicting credit card defaults are credit score, credit limit utilization and number of days employed. - Age of the borrower is not a significant factor in predicting credit card defaults highlighting the importance of considering other variables when analyzing credit risk. - By using machine learning techniques such as XGBoost,Random Forest, Decision Tree, LightGBM, KNN, CatBoost financial institutions can better identify and manage credit risk, thereby reducing their losses due to defaults.
